note_data.title: Coordination
keyPoints: **Neurons** transmit electrical impulses as action potentials along their axons
**Resting potential** is -70mV maintained by sodium-potassium pump which actively transports 3Na+ out and 2K+ in
**Action potential** occurs when threshold potential (-55mV) is reached causing voltage-gated sodium channels to open
**Depolarisation** happens when Na+ ions rush into the axon making the inside positive (+40mV)
**Repolarisation** occurs when K+ channels open and K+ ions leave the axon, restoring negative charge inside
**Synapses** transmit impulses using neurotransmitters which diffuse across the synaptic cleft
**Endocrine system** uses hormones transported in blood plasma to target organs with specific receptors
**Hormones** are chemical messengers that bind to complementary receptor proteins on target cells
**Negative feedback** maintains homeostasis by detecting deviations from set point and triggering corrective responses

definitions.definition: A rapid change in potential difference across the axon membrane from -70mV to +40mV caused by movement of sodium and potassium ions
A junction between two neurons where neurotransmitters are released from presynaptic neuron and bind to receptors on postsynaptic neuron
A chemical messenger produced by endocrine glands and transported in blood plasma to target organs where it binds to specific receptors
The minimum depolarisation (-55mV) required to trigger opening of voltage-gated sodium channels and initiate an action potential

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events in logical order with linking words

modelAnswer.question: Explain how an action potential is transmitted along an unmyelinated axon. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Stimulus causes depolarisation of axon membrane. When threshold potential (-55mV) is reached, voltage-gated sodium channels open. Sodium ions rush into axon down concentration gradient making inside positive (+40mV). Local currents spread to adjacent regions of membrane causing depolarisation to spread. Voltage-gated potassium channels open and potassium ions leave axon. Sodium-potassium pump actively transports 3Na+ out and 2K+ in to restore resting potential (-70mV).
markBreakdown: Mark 6: Sodium-potassium pump restores resting potential

commonMistakes: Never say 'nerve impulse travels' — always say 'action potential propagates along axon'
Students often confuse depolarisation with repolarisation - remember depolarisation is becoming less negative
Don't say hormones 'target specific organs' without mentioning complementary receptors
Never describe synapses as 'gaps' — they are junctions with synaptic cleft
Students forget that sodium-potassium pump uses ATP and transports 3Na+ out, 2K+ in
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 - learn the voltage values (-70mV, -55mV, +40mV)
Always include ion movements and voltage changes when describing action potentials
Synapse questions often ask for neurotransmitter release mechanism - mention calcium ions
Hormone questions require mention of blood transport and complementary receptors for full marks
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Action potential graph showing resting potential (-70mV), threshold (-55mV), depolarisation peak (+40mV), repolarisation and refractory period over time. Label voltage-gated Na+ and K+ channel opening/closing points.
